reading_plan.reading_goal: Reconstruct the research evolution trajectory of engineering fast-folding GFP variants using secretory pathway quality control. Identify the initial problem (GFP folding inefficiency), constraints (need for a screen/selection), hypotheses (SecYEG quality control can discriminate folding), experimental designs (cytoplasmic vs. periplasmic expression, library construction, flow cytometry), observations (frGFP vs. sfGFP behavior, SecYEG rejection of misfolded proteins), failures/partial results (cytoplasmic screen insufficient, initial library screening outcomes), revisions (switching to SecYEG-mediated selection, mutagenesis strategies), validations (biochemical characterization, folding kinetics, stability), and limitations.

selected_sections.reason: Abstract provides a concise summary of the problem, approach, key results, and conclusions, essential for understanding the overall trajectory.
Introduction outlines the background, existing GFP variants, folding challenges, and the rationale for using secretory pathway quality control, setting up the research problem and hypotheses.
This section describes the initial goal and experiments to differentiate frGFP and sfGFP, likely revealing early observations, constraints, and the need for a better screen.

Core section detailing the exploitation of SecYEG-mediated quality control for directed evolution, including library design, screening, and identification of improved variants, capturing the main experimental design and results.
Characterization of evolved variants provides validation of folding enhancement through biochemical and biophysical assays, confirming the success and revealing any limitations.
Discussion interprets the results, places them in context, addresses limitations, and suggests future directions, crucial for understanding the broader impact and remaining challenges.

Hypothesis: SecYEG secretory pathway quality control can discriminate folding, enabling selection of superfolded variants.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Superfolder GFP (sfGFP) escapes SecYEG-mediated elimination, unlike other well-folded GFPs. This quality control was used to engineer superfolded GFPmut2 variants with faster folding kinetics, including superfast GFP, without loss of function.
process_signals: sfGFP spared from SecYEG elimination
Hypothesis: SecYEG quality control can be used for engineering superfolded proteins
Combinatorial library of GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG
Isolated variants with enhanced folding, faster kinetics, no loss in fluorescence
methods: Targeting GFP variants to SecYEG translocase

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: GFP folds inefficiently in E. coli. Existing superfolder and supercharged GFPs show improved properties. The lag in engineering such variants suggests screening limitations. The study aims to use SecYEG quality control to evolve superfolded proteins.
process_signals: GFP folding inefficiency problem
Existing superproteins (sfGFP, supercharged GFP) show improved folding
Lag in engineering suggests screening limitations
Hypothesis: cellular quality control can exert evolutionary pressure to improve folding
SecYEG quality control discriminates folded states
failures_or_limits: Screening methods may be limiting progress

section_gists.heading: Exploring the use of cellular folding quality control to identify superfolded proteins
section_gists.gist: Cytoplasmic expression of frGFP and sfGFP showed only 65% fluorescence increase, insufficient for FACS. SecYEG targeting yielded 24-67 fold difference due to cytoplasmic accumulation of sfGFP and degradation of frGFP. Tat pathway gave modest difference.
process_signals: Initial goal: develop cellular screen to differentiate frGFP and sfGFP
Cytoplasmic screen insufficient (only 65% increase)
Tat pathway gave modest difference
SecYEG targeting gave 24-67 fold fluorescence difference
sfGFP accumulates in cytoplasm when targeted to SecYEG; frGFP degraded
Differential cytoplasmic stability enables discrimination
methods: Targeting to Tat and SecYEG pathways
failures_or_limits: Tat pathway gave only modest difference

section_gists.heading: Exploiting SecYEG-mediated quality control to engineer folding-enhanced proteins
section_gists.gist: GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG showed low fluorescence. A combinatorial library of GFPmut2 was created by error-prone PCR and screened via FACS. Nine variants (P1-P9) with increased fluorescence were isolated, containing recurring substitutions. TrxA fusion also increased fluorescence.
process_signals: GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG is 46-fold less fluorescent than sfGFP
First approach: TrxA fusion increased fluorescence, confirming folding trap hypothesis
Library construction: error-prone PCR of GFPmut2, fused to ssDsbA
FACS screening: two rounds, sorting top fluorescent cells
Nine variants (P1-P9) isolated with increased fluorescence
Recurring substitutions identified: N105D, A206V, etc.
Variants accumulate in cytoplasm
failures_or_limits: Initial GFPmut2-SecYEG fluorescence too low for direct screening

section_gists.heading: Characterization of the folding-enhanced GFPmut2 variants
section_gists.gist: Variants P4, P5, P7 showed increased fluorescence and cytoplasmic accumulation. Without signal peptide, they retained fluorescence. Refolding kinetics: P4, P5 comparable to sfGFP; P7 (superfast GFP) faster. All showed increased stability to GdnHCl. Soluble yield slightly lower.
process_signals: Re-transformation confirmed increased fluorescence
Without signal peptide, variants retain fluorescence activity
P4, P5, P7 chosen for detailed characterization
Refolding: P4, P5 comparable to sfGFP; P7 faster (superfast GFP)
All variants show increased stability to chemical denaturation
Soluble yield slightly lower than GFPmut2
Mutations N105D, A206V do not affect folding kinetics
failures_or_limits: frGFP showed modest stability, not expected

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: SecYEG quality control enables evolution of superfolded proteins. Cytoplasmic accumulation depends on kinetic competition between folding and degradation. Overflow due to SRP saturation may contribute. Screening methods, not inherent difficulty, limited previous engineering. Single round of mutagenesis and two FACS rounds yielded superfolded variants.
process_signals: SecYEG quality control successfully used for evolution
Cytoplasmic accumulation linked to folding kinetics and SRP saturation
Overflow substrates may accumulate if they fold fast
Need to optimize promoter/induction to avoid saturating quality control
Screening methods were limiting, not inherent difficulty
Single mutagenesis round and two FACS rounds sufficient
failures_or_limits: Overflow may saturate quality control; need optimization

events.summary: Cytoplasmic expression of frGFP and sfGFP showed only a small fluorescence increase, insufficient for FACS-based directed evolution.
Hypothesis that SecYEG-mediated quality control can discriminate folding and be used to engineer superfolded proteins.
Targeted sfGFP and frGFP to SecYEG via different signal peptides and observed large fluorescence differences.
sfGFP accumulates in cytoplasm when targeted to SecYEG, while frGFP is degraded, enabling discrimination.
GFPmut2 targeted to SecYEG via ssDsbA was 46-fold less fluorescent than sfGFP, providing a low baseline for selection.
Fusion of TrxA to GFPmut2 increased fluorescence, confirming that rapid folding can trap protein in cytoplasm.

events.summary: Performed two rounds of FACS screening and isolated 9 colonies with fluorescent phenotypes above the continuum distribution.
Sequenced variants and identified 7 recurring substitutions, including N105Y, F64L, E124V, K101E, A206V, G232D, V163A.
Re-transformed plasmids P1-P9 into fresh cells and confirmed increased fluorescence and cytoplasmic accumulation.
Subcloned variants without signal peptide and observed little loss in fluorescence, indicating folding improvement without functional loss.
Characterized P4, P5, P7: slightly lower soluble yield and fluorescence, but refolding kinetics comparable to or faster than sfGFP; P7 renamed superfast GFP.
Kinetic unfolding revealed superfast GFP and P5 unfold slowly, P4 unfolds like GFPmut2; variants isolated primarily on improved folding kinetics.
